summary: This guest post by Marc Joffe argues that new federal banking regulations requiring large banks to hold high-quality liquid assets (HQLAs) illogically exclude municipal bonds while allowing riskier assets like foreign government securities, corporate bonds, and stocks. The author contends this will force banks to divest from municipal bonds, reducing demand and raising interest rates for infrastructure financing. This directly contradicts the Obama administration's stated commitment to infrastructure improvement. Joffe presents data showing municipal bonds have lower default rates than foreign government debt and comparable liquidity to corporate bonds, making the exclusion arbitrary and counterproductive.
key_points: New HQLA rules allow banks to hold foreign government bonds, corporate bonds, and stocks, but exclude US municipal bonds despite their lower default rates
No state has defaulted on a general obligation bond since 1933, while foreign governments like Greece have imposed significant losses on bondholders
Corporate bonds trade even less frequently than municipal bonds, undermining regulators' liquidity justification for exclusion
The regulation creates the absurd situation where banks can hold securities from municipal bond insurers but not the safer insured municipal bonds themselves
Municipal bond issuance is already depressed, and this rule will further increase borrowing costs for infrastructure projects
money_quotes: Obama administration officials frequently talk about the need to improve the nation's infrastructure. Yet new regulations published by the Federal Reserve, FDIC and OCC run counter to this policy by limiting the market for municipal bonds.
No state has defaulted on a general obligation bond since 1933.
Bonds issued by foreign governments can and do default. For example, private investors took a 70% haircut when Greek debt was restructured in 2012.
The average corporate bond trades less than once per day. Not very liquid.

Under the new rules, a bank can purchase bonds or stock issued by Assured Guaranty or MBIA – two major municipal bond insurers – but they can't buy state and local government bonds insured by those companies.
Mandating that banks hold safe securities is a good thing; now we need a more data-driven definition of just what safe means.
key_insight: Banking regulations meant to ensure stability are undermining infrastructure investment by arbitrarily excluding low-risk municipal bonds while permitting demonstrably riskier assets, revealing a disconnect between stated policy goals and regulatory implementation.
